speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're exploring the parts of GitHub that make it feel less like a pile of repositories and more like a real community. That includes how you present yourself, how people support open source work, how projects organize documentation, how organizations structure collaboration, how repository templates help people start new work, and the social signals that help you discover people and projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not just about code files and pull requests.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It's also about visibility, trust, discovery, and participation. And for our audience, we also need to talk about how these features behave with screen readers, keyboard navigation, and low-vision workflows.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with your public presence. GitHub has a special profile customization feature that surprises a lot of people. If you create a public repository whose name exactly matches your username, with the same capitalization, the README from that repository is shown right on your GitHub profile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's the special profile README.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. It's basically your introduction page on GitHub. When someone visits your profile, they don't just see your avatar and a short bio. They can also see the content of that README as regular page content.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that matters because a profile is often the first thing someone checks before deciding whether to follow you, collaborate with you, or trust your contributions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It helps answer simple human questions. Who are you? What are you working on? What are you learning? How can someone reach you?

speaker: JAMIE
text: Walk me through creating one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Create a new repository. Name it exactly the same as your GitHub username. Make it public. Initialize it with a README. Then edit that README with the information you want visitors to see on your profile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should go in it?

speaker: ALEX
text: A short introduction is a great start. Then maybe your current focus, like technologies you're learning or projects you're contributing to. You can include skills, though that part is optional. You can add contact information such as email, LinkedIn, or a personal site. And if you want, a fun fact or two. That last piece can make you feel like a real person instead of a résumé in Markdown.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So something like: Hi, I'm Jane Smith. I care about accessibility. I'm learning TypeScript. I contribute to NVDA docs. Here's how to contact me.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The source gave an example like that, with sections for current focus, skills, contact information, and a fun fact. The bigger lesson is structure. Keep it scannable and honest.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What makes a good profile README instead of an overwhelming one?

speaker: ALEX
text: A few best practices. Keep it concise, because most visitors skim. Update it occasionally so it doesn't look abandoned. Be authentic rather than stuffing it with buzzwords. Include useful links so people can learn more.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what should people avoid?

speaker: ALEX
text: Excessive badges. A wall of fifty technology badges can create visual clutter and a lot of screen reader noise. Also be cautious with auto-generated stats widgets. Those commit charts and flashy profile cards are often inaccessible, sometimes poorly labeled, and not always very meaningful.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So a simple profile is better than a noisy one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very often, yes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make the accessibility part explicit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. Use headings, especially level-two headings, so screen reader users can jump by heading with a key like H. If you include images, provide alt text. Avoid ASCII art, because screen readers read it character by character and it becomes frustrating fast. And test your README with a screen reader before you publish it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about low-vision considerations?

speaker: ALEX
text: Profile READMEs respect GitHub's dark and light themes, so test in both. Make sure contrast stays readable and that decorative elements don't become distracting.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Your profile has more than just the README, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can pin up to six repositories, which is a great way to highlight your best work or the projects you want people to notice first. There's also your contribution graph, which shows activity over the past year. You can't really customize that, but it does reflect consistent activity like commits, pull requests, and issues. And you can set a temporary status message, like saying you're away until a certain date.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's useful for expectation-setting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's shift from self-presentation to supporting others: GitHub Sponsors.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is GitHub's way of letting people financially support open source maintainers and projects.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Think of it as a funding layer for open source work. A developer or project can create a Sponsors profile. Then supporters choose a monthly tier or sometimes a custom amount. The important point in the source is that GitHub sends the money directly to the developer and does not take a cut.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why would someone sponsor a project?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three main reasons were highlighted. Sustainability, because many maintainers are volunteers and funding helps them continue. Gratitude, because a tool may have saved you hours of effort. And sometimes sponsor benefits, like priority support, early access, or special community channels.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you sponsor someone?

speaker: ALEX
text: Go to their profile or repository page. Look for the Sponsor button, usually with a heart icon near the top, close to the profile photo area or near the repository controls. Choose a tier or amount, pick a payment method like credit card or PayPal, and complete the process. GitHub sends a receipt, and your sponsorship can appear publicly on your profile if you choose.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for screen reader users?

speaker: ALEX
text: The source specifically notes that the Sponsor control is a standard button. On a page, you can move through buttons with the B key in many screen readers until you hear Sponsor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can regular contributors apply to receive sponsorships too?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you maintain a project or contribute consistently, you can go to the GitHub Sponsors area, join the waitlist or set up sponsors, connect a payout method such as Stripe or a bank account, create sponsorship tiers, and promote that page. The text specifically notes that many accessibility advocates use Sponsors to support work on assistive technology and inclusive design.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next up: wikis. These can be a little confusing because some projects use them heavily and others ignore them.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's because a GitHub wiki is one documentation option, not the only one. A wiki is a separate Markdown-based documentation space attached to a repository. It's useful when you want multi-page docs, tutorials, FAQs, or information that doesn't belong in the main README.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a README is getting too long, a wiki might make sense.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Also if you want community-editable documentation. Wikis can be a lightweight place for shared knowledge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When should you not use a wiki?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the project already uses GitHub Pages or another docs site, a wiki may be redundant. If you need documentation changes to be version-controlled right alongside code, a wiki may be a poor fit because it is a separate Git repository. And if you want strong customization or tighter review control, a wiki is more limited.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you access one?

speaker: ALEX
text: Go to the repository and open the Wiki tab. If the project has no wiki yet, GitHub may show an option to create the first page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And creating pages?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the Wiki tab, choose New page, add a title and Markdown content, then save. Pages automatically appear in the wiki sidebar for navigation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Anything special for accessibility?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The wiki editor is basically the same kind of editor used for issues and pull request comments, so standard Markdown works well. Use headings and lists for structure. Link between wiki pages with double bracket syntax like page title references. And there's one important caveat: because the wiki is its own Git repository, changes pushed directly to the wiki are not governed by the main repository's branch protection rules. In practice that means wiki edits may bypass the review process you expect for code. So treat wikis as supplementary, community-editable docs rather than the place for your most critical, tightly controlled documentation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's pivot into structural features that shape how repositories are organized: templates, organizations, and settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the part that helps contributors understand how projects are set up behind the scenes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. As a contributor, you won't always manage these features, but you'll run into them constantly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with repository templates. What problem do they solve?

speaker: ALEX
text: A template repository is a starter repository. Instead of copying files manually or forking a project when you don't want shared history, you can create a new repository from the template. It gives you the structure, files, and often default workflows you need to begin.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So how is that different from a fork?

speaker: ALEX
text: That distinction matters a lot. A fork keeps a relationship to the original repository. It preserves history and signals that your repository is derived from that upstream project, often because you may contribute back. A template gives you a starting snapshot to build your own independent repository. Usually there is no ongoing fork relationship.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I'm building my own course project from a starter kit, use a template. If I'm planning to contribute changes back to someone else's codebase, fork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Precisely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does a repository become a template?

speaker: ALEX
text: A maintainer enables the template setting in repository settings. Once it's marked as a template, GitHub can offer a Use this template button.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And to use it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the template repository, choose Use this template, then create a new repository from it. Depending on the interface, you may choose whether to include all branches or just the default branch, though many learners will mainly use the default branch option.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Is there an accessibility note here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The source explicitly calls for a screen reader path, so the key practical idea is that the Use this template control is on the repository page and can be reached through standard heading, button, and landmark navigation. For blind users, it's helpful to navigate to the main repository controls area and listen for Use this template.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now organizations. A lot of major open source projects live under organization accounts instead of personal accounts.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. An organization is a shared account structure used by companies, teams, nonprofits, and open source communities. Instead of one person owning everything, the organization owns repositories and manages access through membership and teams.

speaker: JAMIE
text: From a contributor's point of view, why does that matter?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because the org structure explains who maintains the project, who has permissions, and how collaboration is grouped. You might be invited to join an organization, or you may just contribute to repositories it owns. Either way, understanding the model helps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does joining work?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually an owner invites you. You accept the invitation, and then you become a member. Your membership may have certain permissions or team assignments depending on the organization's setup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the visibility of that membership can vary, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Membership visibility can be public or private. Public membership means people can see on your profile that you belong to the organization. Private membership hides that affiliation from public view.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That has privacy implications.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. Some people want their affiliations visible for networking or credibility. Others prefer not to list every organization publicly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about teams inside organizations?

speaker: ALEX
text: Teams are subgroups within an organization. They can represent maintainers, documentation writers, accessibility reviewers, release managers, or any other functional grouping. Teams help control access and make notifications more targeted.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if I'm just browsing an organization page, what should I expect?

speaker: ALEX
text: You'll typically find the organization profile, repositories, perhaps pinned projects, member information if visible, and sometimes teams or package info depending on your permissions. For contributors, this page is a helpful map of a project ecosystem.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Repository settings may sound administrative, but contributors still need to understand them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. A few settings shape your experience directly. First, repository visibility. A repository can be public, private, or in some cases internal within enterprise contexts. Public means anyone can view it. Private means access is restricted.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Second, archived repositories.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. An archived repository is effectively read-only. You can still inspect the code and issues, but active development has stopped. As a contributor, seeing that a repo is archived tells you not to expect merges or ongoing maintenance.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about repository topics?

speaker: ALEX
text: Topics are labels attached to repositories that help with discovery. They might say things like accessibility, screen-reader, docs, python, good-first-issue themes, or framework names. Topics make repositories easier to find via search and topic browsing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the default branch name?

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because many instructions assume a branch name like main. The default branch is the branch GitHub treats as primary. Pull requests often target it, cloned repositories often check it out first, and some workflows depend on it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now for the broader social layer: stars, watching, following, feeds, discovery tools, lists, and searching for inclusive projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is where GitHub starts to feel a bit like a professional social network, but for code and contribution.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. GitHub is more than code hosting. It's a community system.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with stars.

speaker: ALEX
text: A star is basically a bookmark plus a public signal of appreciation. When you star a repository, you're saying this project matters to me, or I want to come back to it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why does star count matter to projects?

speaker: ALEX
text: Stars are an imperfect metric, but they can still indicate visibility, interest, and community recognition. A high star count can attract more contributors or users. But it does not prove quality by itself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you star a repo?

speaker: ALEX
text: On GitHub.com, open the repository and activate the Star button near the top. Keyboard users can tab to it. Screen reader users can navigate by buttons or landmarks until they hear Star. There may also be a keyboard shortcut in some contexts. And with GitHub CLI, you can star a repository from the command line.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source also expects specific CLI coverage.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Conceptually, the CLI supports starring a repository, unstarring it, listing your starred repositories, and checking whether you've starred a particular repository. Even if learners don't memorize command syntax here, they should know those actions exist.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And viewing your stars on the web?

speaker: ALEX
text: That gives you a bookmark list of projects you care about. It becomes more useful over time if you're intentional.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How is watching different from starring?

speaker: ALEX
text: Watching controls notifications. Starring is mostly bookmarking and signaling. Watching says, keep me informed about activity in this repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I want updates, I watch. If I just want to save it, I star.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What watch levels should people know about?

speaker: ALEX
text: There are different notification levels, such as all activity, custom levels in the web interface, releases only, and ignore. The key idea is that watching is granular. You can choose to be highly informed, lightly informed, or explicitly ignore activity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can all of that be done from the CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: Not equally. The source points out that some granular watch levels, like releases only, require the GitHub web interface and are not available via API alone. The web UI is where you'll typically choose nuanced watch behavior. The CLI still supports actions like watching a repository for activity, ignoring a repository, listing watched repositories, and stopping watching.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a good example of a place where the website still matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much so.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next is following people.

speaker: ALEX
text: Following is about people rather than repositories. When you follow someone, GitHub may surface their public activity in your experience. It helps you build a network around maintainers, educators, accessibility advocates, or contributors whose work you respect.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Who should learners consider following?

speaker: ALEX
text: People whose work you learn from, projects you contribute to, communities aligned with your goals, and individuals active in accessibility or inclusive open source. Following can become a discovery path.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you follow someone?

speaker: ALEX
text: Visit their profile and activate the Follow button. Screen reader users can use button navigation to find it. The CLI also supports following a user, unfollowing, listing who you follow, listing your followers, checking whether you follow a specific person, and seeing who another user follows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last one is underrated.

speaker: ALEX
text: It really is. Looking at who respected contributors follow can help you discover more of the community.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's the home feed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Your home feed is what you see when you log in. It may contain activity from repositories you watch, people you follow, recommendation signals, and other discovery content. Think of it as a personalized activity stream.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's not just noise. It can actually help you discover opportunities.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Your feed can show new repositories, releases, issues, or people that matter to your interests. And over time, you can shape it by deciding who to follow and what to watch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Customizing your feed is really customizing your inputs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about Explore?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Explore is a discovery area for finding new repositories, topics, collections, and developers. It helps you move beyond the projects you already know.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Any accessibility guidance?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. When using Explore with a screen reader, rely on headings, landmarks, and structured navigation because Explore pages can be visually dense. Move section by section rather than linearly scanning everything.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Trending?

speaker: ALEX
text: Trending shows what's popular right now, often with filters by language and time period. There's also a developers view in some interfaces, so you can discover active people as well as repositories.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can the CLI show official trending data?

speaker: ALEX
text: Not directly through an official API endpoint. The source notes that trending repositories are not in the official API, but you can approximate them by searching for recently starred popular repositories, including within a specific language. That's a useful reminder that CLI and API access sometimes lag behind the full web experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Topics are another big discovery tool.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. Topics let you browse repositories by category. If you care about accessibility, screen readers, docs, educational resources, or beginner-friendly projects, topics can quickly narrow your search.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source also calls out useful topics for this community.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Learners in this audience should watch for topics like accessibility, a11y, screen-reader, inclusive-design, documentation, open-source, good-first-issue, and language-specific topics relevant to their stack.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And with the CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can search for repositories with a specific topic, and if it's your own repo, you can add a topic so others can discover it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: GitHub Lists are newer for some learners. What do they do?

speaker: ALEX
text: Lists let you organize starred repositories into named collections. So instead of one giant pile of stars, you can group repositories by purpose, such as accessibility tools, learning resources, projects to contribute to, or examples to revisit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you manage lists from the CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: The source says lists are managed through the web interface only. The CLI can still help you view stars, but list management itself is a web feature.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's another case where the browser remains important.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I'm glad this appendix includes a section on finding accessible and inclusive projects, because that's especially relevant for our audience.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Search intentionally. Look for repositories focused on accessibility. Search for good first issues in accessibility-related projects. Search for issues labeled both accessibility and help wanted. Combine topic filters and issue filters when possible.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And beyond raw search?

speaker: ALEX
text: Follow organizations known for accessibility work. Pay attention to who your community follows. If a trusted accessibility advocate follows certain projects or maintainers, that's often a strong signal.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So social discovery and technical search work best together.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a section about building your own presence, which kind of brings us full circle.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. Your contribution graph tells part of your story, though it should not become a source of anxiety. Pin repositories that represent the kind of work you want people to notice. Keep your profile README current enough to reflect who you are now. And make activity choices intentionally.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning?

speaker: ALEX
text: Meaning consistent, meaningful contribution beats performative busyness. Open issues thoughtfully. Improve docs. Review pull requests when appropriate. Make small contributions regularly instead of obsessing over streaks or vanity metrics.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap, let's call out the CLI angle one more time, because the appendix bundles a lot of social actions with GitHub CLI.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. Learners should know the CLI can help with following and unfollowing users, listing followers and following relationships, starring and unstarring repositories, listing stars, and some discovery searches. It can also help with some watch-related actions, though not every granular notification preference is exposed there. The general rule is this: if you prefer the command line, GitHub CLI supports a meaningful chunk of social and discovery workflows, but the web interface still matters for rich navigation, Explore, Lists, and certain watch settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for screen reader users, the appendix also included practical navigation guidance.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For following a user, navigate to the user profile and find the Follow button using button or landmark navigation. For starring a repository, find the Star button near the top controls. For browsing your stars, use heading and list navigation to move through saved repositories. For exploring topics, jump by headings and links. And for Explore and Trending, expect dense pages and navigate by sections rather than trying to read everything in sequence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a great reminder that accessibility strategy is not just about whether something is technically labeled. It's also about how to move through complex interfaces efficiently.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if we zoom out, what's the big takeaway?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub is not only where code lives. It's where people introduce themselves, support maintainers, document projects, structure communities, discover opportunities, and build long-term professional relationships. Understanding profiles, sponsors, wikis, templates, organizations, repository settings, and social features helps you contribute more effectively and participate more confidently.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you care about accessible contribution, these features are not side details. They're part of how people find your work, understand your project, and decide whether your space is welcoming.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. Build a profile that's readable. Support the work you depend on if you can. Use wikis carefully. Learn the difference between templates and forks. Understand how organizations shape collaboration. Use stars, watches, topics, follows, and lists intentionally. And always keep accessibility in view.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's our lesson for today.

speaker: ALEX
text: Thanks for listening.
